author: @TrentTationnaiseXization
text: I was playing with a simular idea just yesterday. Thin sheets of magnetic materials are easily heated to the point where they are no longer attracted by magnetism and once cooled off again will be attracted. One could imagine many ways to manage these interactions.

author: @chuffpup
text: The spinning has nothing to do with sunlight, and is only the result of heat, any heat will do but as you will notice it only spins as long as you keep moving the lens around. It's the movement that coaxes the graphite to spin and if it was held still nothing would happen. It's the same as that trick with the spinning ball bearings and magnets on the table top, not free energy, and did he say he was selling the magic graphite?

replies.author: @michaelfallentreewatts1398
replies.text:
Yeah I just saw the sun today. 

Just wondering, since you seem intelligent, you know of any great ways to warm a ten by ten ft room to really save on energy?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Michael Watts
I can think of a few ways - but it depends on how much effort and technology you want to put into it. There seems to be a bit of a trade off between expense of fuel versus expense of equipment - after all a fire is pretty cheap but the fuel costs whereas a ground source heat pump uses very little energy but installing the technology is pretty expensive

replies.author: @michaelfallentreewatts1398
replies.text: I'm really looking for something like another version of a radiator style heater. Right now I am completely off grid and want new heat. Maybe ways to get more heat off just a candle?

author: @Theyesac18
text: could it be possible that the focused energy from the fresnel lens is blasting micro-particulate graphite off at high speeds, and because of the twisted magnetic field induced the micro particles are accelerated not directly off but at the correct angle to cause rotation? Just a thought, I may be way off but hey, that's science.

author: @RyanBellRyanofWeird
text: That is cool. Now, if I build that here in America - I wonder if it would spin in the other direction?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i never thought about that - maybe it would lol

replies.author: @Valkyrie612
replies.text: Good question. The direction of spin depends on the initial angle of acceleration caused by the sunlight. In other words, it depends on where on the graphite chip the sunlight is concentrated.

author: @SkyKing101010
text:
That really is very cool.
If that could be done on a larger scale and somehow control the light or magnetic field, or both, the applications could be staggering. I've already thought of 2 or 3 possibilities and I haven't even tried yet.

author: @SkyKing101010
text:
Have you considered that he's working with graphite? By nature, it smudges on everything it touches, so it's not surprising that it's under his finger nails.
One thing you might try is thinking before speaking. That would work very well for everyone. I don't like being snarky, but I'm tired of people being rude without thinking.
Have a nice day.

author: @JustinHallPlus
text: If you made layers of graphite with a dielectric separator between the layers (maybe paper soaked in oil, or PPG or PEG) with the layers punched out to the correct size, do you think you could create a heat driven magnetic motor?  I mean, you've got a proof of concept right here, and I'm sure you've been considering ways to scale this up and apply it.  I can't believe this isn't man made pyrolytic carbon.

How much fun is that ! being at the place that sent you the stuff..lots of learning I bet.

Now - here's the idea while you have a gajillion tons of it around. Make a 10ft long bed of magnets of all the same polarity upward, about 3ft wide. Can be an electromagnet if easier. author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: The heat from the sunlight changes the diamagnetic properties of the graphite and so disturbs the equilibrium of the system. But as the diamagnetic graphite is very very thermally conductive as soon as it moves out of the heat spot the heat is dissipated across the surface and lost rapidly. This continues and the disk spins

author: @IndianaBoys
text: Dr. Murray-Smith I presume? :)  I see you are on a great track in soon understanding the natural process and materials used to reassemble diamagnetic pieces in the lab!  Good job mate!  Arthur C. Clark quote "Science demands patience."  Thanks for continuing to provide real world eductaional videos!
